title: Analyzing Artifacts and File Systems
content:
{"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"imageBlock","attrs":{"src":"/assets/b93e2e45-a425-4ed9-b87e-857292cf6569","alt":"Analyzing Artifacts and File Systems","caption":"","type":"url"}},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Listen up. If you're reading this, it means someone compromised a system, and you're the one stuck digging through the digital garbage to figure out exactly what happened. This isn't a hand-holding seminar. We are parsing raw file systems, ripping out hidden artifacts, and building event timelines that actually hold up under scrutiny."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Stop relying on shiny GUI tools that do the thinking for you. If you don't understand the underlying structures, you're going to miss the obvious, ruin the evidence, and embarrass yourself. Pay attention."}]},{"type":"infoBlock","attrs":{"id":"info-1776963311958-7lkrfp53c","type":"danger"},"content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Rule Number One:"},{"type":"text","text":" If you mount the original evidence drive in read-write mode, hand in your badge and find a new career. Always use a write-blocker, and always work from a forensic image. No exceptions."}]}]},{"type":"heading","attrs":{"level":2},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"File System Parsing: Stop Relying on Point-and-Click"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"You need to know how to navigate a file system at the block level. If you can't extract a deleted file using raw inodes or Master Filasase Table (MFT) records, you're just guessing. We use The Sleuth Kit (TSK) because it actually works and doesn't hide the details from you."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"First, identify the partition layout. If you can't find the starting sector, you can't parse the volume."}]},{"type":"codeBlock","attrs":{"language":"bash"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"# Don't just stare at it. Find the NTFS or ext4 partition offset.
mmls evidence.E01"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Once you have the offset (let's say it's "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"code"}],"text":"2048"},{"type":"text","text":"), list the files. And yes, this includes the deleted ones that the attacker thought they wiped."}]},{"type":"codeBlock","attrs":{"language":"bash"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"# The -o flag is your offset. The -r flag is recursive. 
# If you forget them, that's on you.
fls -o 2048 -r evidence.E01"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"When you find a suspicious inode (e.g., "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"code"}],"text":"12345-128-1"},{"type":"text","text":"), rip the raw data out immediately."}]},{"type":"codeBlock","attrs":{"language":"bash"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"icat -o 2048 evidence.E01 12345-128-1 > extracted_malware.bin"}]},{"type":"heading","attrs":{"level":2},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Extracting Hidden Artifacts: Look Deeper"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Attackers hide things. If you're only looking at the active, allocated files in the standard directories, you are doing it wrong. You need to hunt in the dark corners of the file system."}]},{"type":"heading","attrs":{"level":3},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Alternate Data Streams (ADS)"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"On Windows (NTFS), attackers love hiding payloads in Alternate Data Streams. The file size of the parent file won't change, which fools amateurs."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Using TSK, an ADS shows up with a colon in the name (e.g., "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"code"}],"text":"legit.txt:hidden_payload.exe"},{"type":"text","text":"). Look for them, extract them, and hash them."}]},{"type":"heading","attrs":{"level":3},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Volume Shadow Copies (VSS)"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"If the attacker deleted their tracks, check the Volume Shadow Copies. Windows backs up files automatically. Unless the attacker explicitly ran "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"code"}],"text":"vssadmin delete shadows"},{"type":"text","text":", their deleted tools and logs are probably sitting right there waiting for you. Mount the VSS and parse it just like the live system."}]},{"type":"cardBlock","attrs":{"id":"card-1776963311958-gb2ncat6n","title":"Pro Tip: Unallocated Space","icon":"fa fa-search","iconAlign":"left","href":""},"content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Carving unallocated space isn't optional. Use tools like "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"code"}],"text":"photorec"},{"type":"text","text":" or "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"code"}],"text":"bulk_extractor"},{"type":"text","text":" to rip PCAPs, executables, and memory dumps out of the void. If you skip this, you're leaving evidence on the table."}]}]},{"type":"heading","attrs":{"level":2},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Reconstructing Event Timelines"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"If you can't put events in chronological order, you don't have a case; you just have a pile of useless files. We build Super Timelines using Plaso ("},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"code"}],"text":"log2timeline"},{"type":"text","text":"). It parses everything—MFT, event logs, registry hives, browser history—and spits out a single, unified timeline."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Here is exactly how you do it. Do not skip steps."}]},{"type":"stepsBlock","attrs":{"id":"steps-1776963311958-5xqq0mbtw"},"content":[{"type":"stepBlock","attrs":{"title":"Generate the Plaso Storage File"},"content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Run "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"code"}],"text":"log2timeline.py"},{"type":"text","text":" against your forensic image. This will take hours. Go get a coffee and don't interrupt it."}]},{"type":"codeBlock","attrs":{"language":"bash"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"log2timeline.py --storage-file case_timeline.plaso evidence.E01"}]}]},{"type":"stepBlock","attrs":{"title":"Filter the Garbage"},"content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"A raw Plaso file has millions of events. You need to filter it down to the relevant timeframe using "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"code"}],"text":"psort.py"},{"type":"text","text":", or you'll drown in noise."}]},{"type":"codeBlock","attrs":{"language":"bash"},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"psort.py -o l2tcsv -w filtered_timeline.csv case_timeline.plaso 
"date > '2023-10-01 00:00:00' AND date < '2023-10-05 23:59:59'""}]}]},{"type":"stepBlock","attrs":{"title":"Analyze the Output"},"content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Open "},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"code"}],"text":"filtered_timeline.csv"},{"type":"text","text":" in Timeline Explorer or your tool of choice. Look for execution artifacts (Prefetch, Shimcache) immediately preceding network connections or file modifications."}]}]}]},{"type":"heading","attrs":{"level":3},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"The Investigation Flow"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Get this process through your head. If you deviate from this workflow, you will miss the root cause."}]},{"type":"mermaidBlock","attrs":{"id":"mermaid-1776963311958-vq6ar4no4","diagram":"flowchart TD
    A["Acquire Image (Read-Only)"] --> B["Identify Partitions (mmls)"]
    B --> C["Extract MFT & Artifacts"]
    B --> D["Carve Unallocated Space"]
    C --> E["Generate Plaso Timeline"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["Filter by Incident Window"]
    F --> G["Identify Root Cause"]
    
    style A fill:#ff4d4d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style G fill:#4CAF50,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff","title":""}},{"type":"infoBlock","attrs":{"id":"info-1776963311958-s3v1nm2p6","type":"warning"},"content":[{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"text":"Timestamp Manipulation (Timestomping):"},{"type":"text","text":" Attackers will alter file creation dates to mess with your timeline. Always compare the standard information attribute ("},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"code"}],"text":"$SI"},{"type":"text","text":") against the file name attribute ("},{"type":"text","marks":[{"type":"code"}],"text":"$FN"},{"type":"text","text":") in the MFT. If they don't match, someone is lying to you."}]}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Do the work. Validate your findings. Don't make assumptions."}]}]}
